messages.content:
Complete the chart by dragging and dropping the correct meaning into the third column to match the term in each row.
Meaning Options (6 of 6 )
a persuasive technique that appeals to logic and reasoning
I a device that writers use to make their arguments appeal to readers
a writing or speaking technique that helps to make arguments more persuasive the art of speaking and writing persuasively

a persuasive technique that attempts to stir up emotions
a persuasive technique that aims to establish a writer's or speaker's credibility and shared values with the audience ethos
logos
pathos
rhetoric
rhetorical appeal
rhetorical device

Term | Definition | Meaning

rhetoric | the art of speaking and writing persuasively | 

rhetorical device | a device that writers use to make their arguments appeal to readers | 

rhetorical appeal | a writing or speaking technique that helps to make arguments more persuasive | 

ethos | a persuasive technique that aims to establish a writer's or speaker's credibility and shared values with the audience | 

logos | a persuasive technique that appeals to logic and reasoning | 

pathos | a persuasive technique that attempts to stir up emotions |

Free and open debate is what makes this country work, and many people around the world risk their lives every day for the liberties we often take for granted,' Carson said. 'But as much as we value a healthy debate, we don't let that debate tear us apart."
From "California Dreamin': How important is it for America to remain one nation, indivisible?"
"Imagine a delicately carved white sculpture. It depicts beautiful figures, nature and detailed patterns. However, this isn't a typical marble sculpture. This
sculpture is made out of ivory-sourced from the tusks of dead African elephants.
From "Illegal Ivory: How much impact can government action have on the ivory trade?"
"Today, when you think of social media apps like Instagram, how do they really make you feel? In April 2018, Eva Chen, director of fashion partnerships at Instagram, announced that the social media platform had created a 'Wellbeing Team' within the company."
From "Antisocial Media: How important is it to be popular on social media?"
Looking at reading data, we identified students who were a year, two years behind in reading, Liechty told the newspaper. 'From that, we had more than 120 kids. We had to put a cap on it so we could fit under one roof.!"
From ") Promise: How can public figures impact a city?"

Article Title | Main Idea

"California Dreamin': How important is it for America to remain one nation, indivisible?" | The importance of unity in America and the need to balance healthy debate with avoiding division.

"Illegal Ivory: How much impact can government action have on the ivory trade?" | The issue of the illegal ivory trade and the impact of government action on it.

"Antisocial Media: How important is it to be popular on social media?" | The impact of social media on well-being and mental health.

") Promise: How can public figures impact a city?" | The importance of public figures in addressing educational disparities and improving outcomes for students.

Ethos
Logos
Pathos or Kairos

Directions: Read the following excerpts. Then, sort them by the rhetorical appeal each writer uses.
I'll do my best to help you sort the excerpts by the rhetorical appeal! Please provide the excerpts.

1 Lorenz says that teenagers frequently use smartphones. However, they aren't fully paying attention to the content on these devices.
"When you're bored on your phone, you're just sitting with your own thoughts," 17-year-old Maxine Marcus told The Daily Beast. "You're on it, but it's just an action so your brain still goes wherever it wants to go. You get bored and you start thinking and daydreaming."
2 Social media companies work to make sure users stay entertained. However, research suggests that boredom may not actually be as bad as it seems. Boredom can lead to creativity, according to a 2014 study by the University of Central Lancashire. In one experiment, researchers divided people into two groups. The researchers gave one group a "boring" activity. They did not give an activity to the other group. When both groups were asked to complete a creative thinking test afterwards, the bored group performed much better. Why? Sandi Mann, the psychologist who ran the study, believes boredom puts our minds into a state of seeking. It makes our brains work harder.

1 | Pathos 
2 | Logos

1. What is the intended audience?
• A. teenagers
• B. social media company emplovees
2. Which statement best summarizes the author's point of view?
A. "Social media companies work to make sure users stay entertained."
B. "Boredom can lead to creativity, according to a 2014 study by the University of Central Lancashire."
3. What detail best supports the author's point of view?
• A. "Sandi Mann, the psychologist who ran the study, believes boredom puts our minds into a state of seeking."
B. "When both groups were asked to complete a creative thinking test afterwards, the bored group performed much better."
4. What type of rhetorical appeal is the writer using in the following excerpt from paragraph 2?
"When both groups were asked to complete a creative thinking test afterwards, the bored group performed much better."
• A. logos
B. pathos

1 | A. teenagers 
2 | B. "Boredom can lead to creativity, according to a 2014 study by the University of Central Lancashire."
3 | B. "When both groups were asked to complete a creative thinking test afterwards, the bored group performed much better."
4 | A. logos

5. Which detail would improve the author's use of rhetoric in paragraph 2?
© A. Sandi Mann's credentials as well as her previous employers
B. statistics or numerical data from Sandi Mann's 2014 study
6. What type of rhetorical appeal would the writer be using if she included a personal account related to boredom and creativity?
• A. logos
• B. ethos

5 | B. statistics or numerical data from Sandi Mann's 2014 study 
6 | B. ethos

Directions: Read the following excerpt. Then, complete the chart by answering each question.
In this excerpt from his first speech as Prime Minister of Great Britain, Churchill makes an argument about World War Il.
From "Blood, Toil, Tears and Sweat" by Winston Churchill
1 You ask, what is our policy? I can say: It is to wage war, by sea, land and air, with all our might and with all the strength that God can give us; to wage war against a monstrous tyranny, never surpassed in the dark, lamentable catalogue of human crime. That is our policy.
2 You ask, what is our aim? I can answer in one word: It is victory, victory at all costs, victory in spite of all terror, victory, however long and hard the road may be; for without victory, there is no survival. Let that be realised; no survival for the British Empire, no survival for all that the British Empire has stood for, no survival for the urge and impulse of the ages, that mankind will move forward towards its goal. But I take up my task with buoyancy and hope. I feel sure that our cause will not be suffered to fail among men. At this time I feel entitled to claim the aid of all, and I say, "come then, let us go forward together with our united strength."

1. What is being argued in this speech excerpt?
- The need to wage war against a monstrous tyranny.

2. What is the speaker's policy?
- The policy is to wage war, by sea, land, and air, with all the strength that God can give them.

3. What is the speaker's aim?
- The aim is victory at all costs, in spite of all terror, and however long and hard the road may be. It is emphasized that without victory, there is no survival.

4. What details does the speaker use to emphasize the importance of victory?
- The speaker details that without victory, there will be no survival for the British Empire, no survival for what it has stood for, and no survival for the impulse of the ages for mankind to move forward towards its goal.

5. What type of rhetorical appeal is the speaker using?
- Pathos, as the speaker is appealing to the audience's emotions through the emphasis on the need for victory for survival.

What is Churchill's point of view and perspective?
What details does the Churchill give in support of his point of view and perspective?
What type of appeal does the author use in his argument?
What is Churchill asking of Britain's people? Support your response with evidence from the text.
Is Churchill's use of rhetorical appeals effective? Why or why not?
Support your response with evidence from the text.

1. Churchill's point of view is that Britain must wage war against a monstrous tyranny in order to avoid the destruction of the British Empire and all that it has stood for.

2. Churchill emphasizes the need for victory at all costs in order to survive, and highlights the urgency of the situation by emphasizing the terrible nature of the enemy Britain must fight against.

3. Churchill uses a combination of rhetorical appeals, including a strong use of pathos to appeal to the emotions of the audience, and ethos to establish himself as a credible and authoritative figure. 

4. Churchill is asking the people of Britain to unite behind the cause of victory, and he emphasizes the need for their support by saying "come then, let us go forward together with our united strength."

5. Churchill's use of rhetorical appeals is effective, as he is able to establish himself as a credible and authoritative figure, appeal to the emotions of the audience, and emphasize the urgency of the situation in order to inspire the people of Britain to support the cause of victory.
resume the answers

1. Churchill's point of view is that Britain must wage war against a monstrous tyranny in order to avoid the destruction of the British Empire and all that it has stood for.

2. Churchill emphasizes the need for victory at all costs in order to survive, and highlights the urgency of the situation by emphasizing the terrible nature of the enemy Britain must fight against. He also cites the impulse of mankind to move forward towards its goal as a reason for the importance of victory.

3. Churchill uses a combination of rhetorical appeals, including a strong use of pathos to appeal to the emotions of the audience, and ethos to establish himself as a credible and authoritative figure. 

4. Churchill is asking the people of Britain to unite behind the cause of victory, and he emphasizes the need for their support by saying "come then, let us go forward together with our united strength."

5. Churchill's use of rhetorical appeals is effective, as he is able to establish himself as a credible and authoritative figure, appeal to the emotions of the audience, and emphasize the urgency of the situation in order to inspire the people of Britain to support the cause of victory. His use of the phrase "victory at all costs" is particularly effective in communicating the gravity of the situation and the need for a united effort.
